summary: Gruber dissects John Paczkowski's interview with Adobe co-founder Chuck Geschke, highlighting the contradictions in Adobe's arguments against Apple's iPhone OS restrictions. He challenges Geschke's claim that cross-platform apps like Photoshop and Acrobat don't suffer quality trade-offs, pointing to well-documented Mac user complaints. Gruber also dismantles Geschke's assertion that HTML isn't standardized, noting it's governed by W3C and WHATWG specifications. He draws a sharp parallel between Adobe's control over Flash and Apple's control over iPhone OS, arguing Adobe's position is fundamentally hypocritical. The piece concludes that Flash is on the web but not of the web, and that Adobe maintains the same kind of platform control it criticizes Apple for exercising.
key_points: Geschke's citation of Photoshop and Acrobat as examples of successful cross-platform apps undermines his own argument, since both are widely criticized by Mac users for poor platform-native quality
Lightroom would have been a far stronger example of cross-platform done right, but Geschke didn't mention it
HTML is standardized through W3C and WHATWG specifications; Geschke's claim otherwise conflates inconsistent rendering with lack of standards
Adobe maintains tight control over Flash while criticizing Apple for controlling iPhone OS, a fundamentally contradictory position
Apple's dual-platform strategy — closed native SDK plus open web standards — represents a coherent philosophy that Adobe's pitch for Flash as a third option fails to match
money_quotes: Acrobat is despised by Mac users. If not for Apple's Preview (and Mac OS X's Apple-written PDF implementation), Mac users would despise the entire PDF format.

I hope for Adobe's sake that he's being disingenuous about these issues; otherwise the co-chairman of their board is completely ignorant of how their flagship products are perceived by many Mac users.
The CS suite apps have evolved to look and feel like Adobe apps, not like Mac or Windows apps.
I wouldn't hesitate to hold up Lightroom as the single best cross-platform app in history.
Flash is associated with web browsers because for the past decade, most web browsers in use include a Flash Player plugin. But Flash is not part of the web. It's on the web, but not of the web.
The problem with HTML as a development platform is that it's standardized in so many different ways.
In other words, Adobe maintains as much control over its own platforms and formats as it sees fit. That sounds pretty much like what Apple is doing with iPhone OS.
key_insight: Adobe's campaign against Apple's platform control is undermined by Adobe's own identical approach to controlling Flash, and by the demonstrable quality costs of its cross-platform strategy that its co-founder refuses to acknowledge.
